steps.title: Understand what the study design controls for
steps.content: Each dog appeared in two iris-color versions (light and dark), but no participant saw both versions of the same dog. This means any difference in ratings can only be attributed to iris color, not to other facial features of the specific dogs used.

steps.title: Eliminate wrong choices and confirm the answer
steps.content:
• Choice A — "may have been different if participants had been able to see the dogs' irises in person instead of only seeing images that included the dogs' irises": concerns in-person versus image viewing — the design does nothing to address this. ✗
  • Choice B — "reflected a preference on the part of participants for facial features that happened to coincide with dark irises in the dogs depicted in the images": using the same dog in both iris conditions rules out the possibility that other facial features (not iris color) drove the preference. ✓
  • Choice C — "were a function of participants emphasizing the colors of the dogs' irises over the dogs' kindness and trustworthiness when reacting to the images": mischaracterizes what the rating task measures — participants rated kindness and trustworthiness; the design does not address what mental process they used. ✗
  • Choice D — "could be explained by participants having preexisting positive feelings about dogs with dark irises or preexisting negative feelings about dogs with light irises": describes preexisting bias, which random assignment of participants to image sets is what would address — not the specific design feature described. ✗
That matches choice B — B is correct.
